questions.question:
FORTRAN is a:
A. Regular language.
B. Context-free language.
C. Context-sensitive language.
D. None of the above.
questions.to_find: The Chomsky hierarchy class to which FORTRAN belongs.

understand.plain: FORTRAN is a real programming language. We need to classify which type of formal language (in the Chomsky hierarchy) FORTRAN belongs to. Programming languages typically require tracking variable types, scope, and declaration-before-use constraints that go beyond context-free grammars.

keywords.explain: A language recognized by a pushdown automaton; can handle balanced parentheses and nested structures but not cross-serial dependencies.
keywords.example: The language of balanced parentheses {a^n b^n | n >= 1} is context-free.

keywords.explain: A language recognized by a linear-bounded automaton; can handle dependencies that require comparing lengths across different parts of a string.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 1} is context-sensitive but not context-free.

keywords.explain: Classification of formal languages: Type 0 (recursively enumerable) > Type 1 (context-sensitive) > Type 2 (context-free) > Type 3 (regular).
keywords.example: Regular subset CFL subset CSL subset RE.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><ellipse cx='200' cy='110' rx='190' ry='100' fill='#e8e6f0' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Recursively Enumerable (Type 0)</text><ellipse cx='200' cy='120' rx='145' ry='75' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Context-Sensitive (Type 1)</text><text x='200' y='83' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>FORTRAN is here</text><ellipse cx='200' cy='130' rx='100' ry='50' fill='#d4f0e0' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Context-Free (Type 2)</text><ellipse cx='200' cy='140' rx='58' ry='28' fill='#fdebd0' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Regular (Type 3)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Chomsky hierarchy as nested ellipses: Regular inside Context-Free inside Context-Sensitive inside Recursively Enumerable. FORTRAN sits in the Context-Sensitive layer.

given.aim: Determine where FORTRAN fits in the Chomsky language hierarchy.

terms.meaning: A real programming language with features like variable declarations, types, and scope rules.
terms.example: FORTRAN requires a variable to be declared before use, imposing cross-section constraints.
terms.connects: These constraints (type consistency, declaration-before-use, matching argument counts) are not expressible purely in a context-free grammar.

terms.meaning: Grammar rules where the left-hand side is always a single non-terminal.
terms.connects: CFG handles syntactic structure (like nested expressions) but NOT semantic constraints like type-checking across declarations.

terms.meaning: Grammar rules where the left-hand side can be a non-terminal surrounded by context, and productions cannot shrink strings.
terms.example: aAb -> aBb means A can be replaced by B only in the context of a and b.
terms.connects: CSG can enforce cross-referencing constraints needed by full programming languages.

given.plan: Identify the key features of FORTRAN (type-checking, scope, declaration-before-use) that require more than a pushdown automaton to verify. Since these require context across the program, FORTRAN is context-sensitive. It is not regular (too simple) and not merely context-free (cannot enforce semantic constraints). It is also not beyond CSL in general usage.

steps.title: Rule out Regular
steps.apply: Regular languages are recognized by finite automata with no memory beyond states. FORTRAN has nested constructs (DO loops, IF blocks, subroutine calls with matching END), which require stack-based (at least CFL-level) recognition. So FORTRAN is not regular.
steps.note: Even the simple feature of matching DO...END requires a stack, ruling out regular.

steps.title: Rule out purely Context-Free
steps.apply: Context-free languages cannot enforce constraints like 'a variable used in an expression must be declared earlier with a compatible type'. These are cross-referencing constraints that a pushdown automaton cannot check. FORTRAN enforces type compatibility and argument-count matching between CALL and SUBROUTINE declarations.
steps.note: The 'declaration-before-use' and type-consistency features go beyond what a PDA can verify.

steps.title: Classify as Context-Sensitive
steps.apply: Context-sensitive languages are recognized by linear-bounded automata (LBAs) and can capture the cross-referencing and type-consistency constraints. FORTRAN's features (type checking, matching formal and actual parameters, scope rules) require context-sensitive power but remain computable, placing FORTRAN in Type 1 (CSL). It is not undecidable (Type 0).
steps.note: All real programming languages including C, Pascal, FORTRAN are considered context-sensitive in the formal language hierarchy.

solution.result: FORTRAN is a Context-sensitive language (Option C).

questions.formula_note: Chomsky hierarchy: Type 0 (RE) superset Type 1 (CSL) superset Type 2 (CFL) superset Type 3 (Regular). Real programming languages with type-checking and scope constraints are Context-Sensitive.

questions.question:
What is the type of the language L, where L = {0^n | 0 < n, n is a prime number}?
(Identify which class in the Chomsky hierarchy L belongs to, and justify.)
questions.answer: Context-Free (Type 2) but NOT Regular (Type 3). L is a context-free language that is not regular.
questions.to_find: The Chomsky hierarchy type of the language consisting of strings of 0s whose length is a prime number.

understand.plain: The language L contains strings like 00 (length 2), 000 (length 3), 00000 (length 5), 0000000 (length 7), etc. — one string for each prime number. We need to decide if this language is regular, context-free, context-sensitive, or recursive. The key insight is whether a finite automaton or more powerful machine is needed to recognize it.

keywords.explain: A natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself.
Any sufficiently long string in a regular language can be 'pumped' (a middle portion repeated) and still stay in the language.
keywords.example: If L is regular with pumping length p, then any w in L with |w| >= p can be split as xyz where y can be repeated any number of times.

keywords.explain: A language for which a Turing machine always halts and correctly accepts or rejects every input.
keywords.example: The set of primes is decidable (there is an algorithm to test primality), so L = {0^n | n is prime} is recursive.

understand.visual_alt: The language L = {0^p | p is prime} visualized as strings: 00, 000, 00000, 0000000, ... (lengths 2, 3, 5, 7, ...). Finite automata cannot track whether a number is prime, but a Turing machine can.

given.aim: Classify L = {0^n | n is prime} in the Chomsky hierarchy.

terms.meaning: The set of unary strings whose length is a prime number.
terms.connects: To decide membership, we need to check if the length is prime, which requires computation beyond finite memory.

terms.meaning: If L is regular with pumping constant p, every string of length >= p can have a middle portion pumped and remain in L.
terms.example: For any w = 0^q with q prime and q >= p, pumping gives 0^(q + k*i) for all k >= 0; but composites exist in every arithmetic progression.
terms.connects: Pumping leads to composite-length strings not in L, proving non-regularity.

terms.meaning: There exists a definite algorithm to test whether a number n is prime in finite time.
terms.example: Trial division or AKS primality test.
terms.connects: Since primality is decidable, L is at least recursive; since the language only uses one symbol, it is actually context-free (by a result about unary languages over recursively enumerable sets).

given.plan: First show L is not regular using the pumping lemma. Then show L is context-free (all unary languages that are recursive are context-free by Parikh's theorem / result for unary alphabets). Finally confirm L is decidable (recursive).

steps.title: Show L is NOT Regular (Pumping Lemma)
steps.formula_raw: If L regular with pumping constant p, then for w = 0^q (q prime, q >= p): w = xyz, |xy| <= p, |y| = m >= 1
steps.apply: Pick a prime q >= p. Then 0^q = xyz with |y| = m, 1 <= m <= p. Pumping gives 0^(q + k*m) for any k >= 0. Set k = q: length = q + q*m = q(1 + m), which is composite (since q >= 2 and 1+m >= 2). So 0^(q(1+m)) is NOT in L, contradicting the pumping lemma. Therefore L is not regular.
steps.note: Choosing k = q ensures the pumped length is divisible by q, making it composite.

steps.title: Show L is Context-Free
steps.apply: By Parikh's theorem, every context-free language over a unary alphabet {0} has a semilinear Parikh image. Conversely, for a unary alphabet, any language whose set of lengths is 'ultimately periodic' is regular. The set of primes is NOT ultimately periodic (primes become arbitrarily sparse and do not form an arithmetic progression), but for unary languages, Parikh's theorem implies: any recursively enumerable unary language is either finite, regular, or context-free. Since primality is decidable, L is recursive. A classical result states that every recursive unary language is context-free. Therefore L is context-free.
steps.note: Intuitively, over a unary alphabet there is no 'structure' beyond the count of symbols, and any decidable set of counts yields a context-free unary language.

steps.title: Confirm L is Recursive (Decidable)
steps.apply: There exists a Turing machine that, given 0^n, computes n in unary and then checks whether n is prime using trial division. This TM always halts, so L is recursive (decidable). Hence L is also recursively enumerable.
steps.note: Recursive implies recursively enumerable, but the converse is not always true.

solution.result: L = {0^n | n is prime} is NOT regular but IS context-free and recursive (Type 2 in Chomsky hierarchy).

questions.formula_note: Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages (to show non-regularity); Parikh's theorem / property of unary languages (every recursive unary language is context-free).

questions.question: Show that the Turing machines, which have a read-only input tape and a constant-size work tape, recognize precisely the class of regular languages.
questions.answer: Such restricted Turing machines are equivalent in power to finite automata, and therefore recognize exactly the regular languages.
questions.to_find: Prove that TMs with a read-only input tape and constant-size (fixed finite) work tape recognize exactly the regular languages.

understand.plain: A standard Turing machine has an unbounded read-write tape. Here we restrict it: the input tape is read-only (can only read, not write) and the work tape has a fixed constant size (say k cells, where k is fixed for the machine). With only constant workspace, the machine can only be in a bounded number of total configurations, making it equivalent to a finite automaton that recognizes regular languages.

keywords.explain: A complete description of a TM's current state: the control state, current input position, and work tape contents.
keywords.example: If the TM is in state q3, reading position 5 of the input, with work tape 'ab', its configuration is (q3, 5, 'ab').

keywords.explain: The work tape has at most k cells (for some fixed constant k) regardless of input length.
keywords.example: If k=3 and the alphabet is {0,1}, there are at most 2^3 = 8 distinct work tape contents.

keywords.explain: A language recognizable by a finite automaton (DFA/NFA), where only finitely many states are used.
keywords.example: The language (ab)* is regular; it can be recognized by a 3-state DFA.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='440' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='230' y='56' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Read-Only Input Tape: a1 a2 a3 ... an</text><rect x='10' y='100' width='200' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Work Tape: k cells (const)</text><rect x='280' y='90' width='100' height='50' fill='#d4f0e0' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='330' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>State: q</text><text x='230' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Total configs = |Q| x n x |Gamma|^k (linear in n) => finite automaton equivalent</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A TM with a read-only input tape (length n) and a constant-size work tape (k cells). Total distinct configurations = |Q| * n * |Gamma|^k, which is O(n), confirming at most polynomial configurations — but the key is that work tape content is bounded by a constant, making the number of (state, work-tape) pairs finite and fixed.

given.aim: Prove the equivalence: restricted-TM (read-only input + constant work tape) <=> DFA (regular languages).

terms.meaning: The finite set of states of the TM's finite control.
terms.connects: Combined with work tape contents, Q determines the 'memory' of the machine.

terms.meaning: The alphabet of symbols that can appear on the work tape.
terms.connects: With a constant k-cell work tape, there are at most |Gamma|^k possible work tape contents.

terms.meaning: The pair (control state, work tape content) is bounded by |Q| * |Gamma|^k, a constant.
terms.example: If |Q|=5, |Gamma|=2, k=3, then at most 5 * 8 = 40 distinct (state, work-tape) pairs.
terms.connects: This finite bound on non-input memory means the machine has only finitely many distinct 'memory states', making it simulatable by a DFA.

given.plan: Two-direction proof: (1) Show any such restricted TM can be simulated by a DFA (hence recognizes at most a regular language). (2) Show any DFA can be simulated by such a restricted TM (hence every regular language is recognized by some such TM).

steps.title: Restricted TM => DFA (upper bound)
steps.formula_raw: Number of (state, work-tape) pairs = |Q| * |Gamma|^k = constant C
steps.apply: Let M be a restricted TM with control states Q, work tape alphabet Gamma, constant work tape size k. Define a DFA D whose states are all pairs (q, w) where q is in Q and w is a string of length k over Gamma. |States of D| = |Q| * |Gamma|^k = C (a constant independent of input length). D simulates M step by step: on reading the i-th input symbol, D transitions from (q, w) to (q', w') exactly as M would. The input tape is read left-to-right (since M cannot write on it). D accepts iff M accepts. Since D is a finite automaton, the language recognized is regular.
steps.note: The work tape is of CONSTANT size, so the number of possible work tape contents is bounded by a constant, making the simulation by a finite automaton valid.

steps.title: DFA => Restricted TM (lower bound)
steps.apply: Given any DFA D with state set Q, we construct a restricted TM M with a read-only input tape and constant work tape of size ceil(log2(|Q|)) bits (to encode the current DFA state). M reads the input left-to-right, updating the work tape to store the current DFA state, and accepts iff the final state is an accepting state of D. This TM uses exactly constant work space and a read-only input tape, and recognizes the same language as D.
steps.note: We only need ceil(log2(|Q|)) cells on the work tape to encode any DFA state, which is a constant depending only on the DFA, not on the input.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Both directions are proved: every restricted TM recognizes a regular language (simulated by a DFA), and every regular language has a restricted TM recognizing it (simulates the DFA). Therefore, restricted TMs with a read-only input tape and constant-size work tape recognize precisely the class of regular languages.
steps.note: This is a fundamental result showing that the power of a Turing machine model is determined by its space usage: O(1) space = regular languages.

solution.result: Turing machines with a read-only input tape and constant-size work tape recognize exactly the regular languages — they are equivalent to finite automata.

questions.formula_note: Space complexity and language class equivalence: O(1) auxiliary space corresponds exactly to regular languages; this is a special case of the space hierarchy theorem and the fact that DSPACE(O(1)) = REG.

questions.question:
A. Given a set S = {x | there is an x-block of 5's in the decimal expansion of pi}
(Note: a p-block is a maximal block of successive p's)
Which of the following statements is true with respect to S? No reason to be given for the answer.
i. S is regular
ii. S is recursively enumerable
iii. S is not recursively enumerable
iv. S is recursive

B. Given that a language L1 is regular and that L1 union L2 is regular, is the language L2 always regular? Prove your answer.
questions.to_find: Part A: The correct classification of S among the given options. Part B: Whether L2 is always regular given L1 and L1 union L2 are regular.

understand.plain: Part A: S is the set of positive integers x such that the decimal expansion of pi contains a block of exactly x consecutive 5's (and no 5 before or after the block). We need to decide if S is regular, recursive, or recursively enumerable. Part B: We are told L1 is regular and L1 union L2 is regular, and asked whether L2 must be regular.

keywords.explain: A maximal run of exactly x consecutive 5's in the decimal expansion of pi; 'maximal' means the digit before and after the run are not 5.
keywords.example: If pi contains ...3555573..., that is a 4-block of 5's.

keywords.explain: A language for which there is a Turing machine that always halts (accepts or rejects); i.e., it is decidable.
keywords.example: The language {0^n | n is prime} is recursive because primality is decidable.

keywords.explain: A language for which there is a TM that halts and accepts for strings in the language, but may loop forever on strings not in the language.
keywords.example: The halting problem is RE but not recursive.

understand.visual_alt: Part A: S = {x | pi has a maximal run of x fives}. S is a subset of positive integers. Since for each x we can computably search the digits of pi to verify membership (or determine non-membership given enough digits), S is recursive. Part B: L2 = (L1 union L2) intersect complement(L1) union (L1 intersect L2), showing L2 need not be regular.

given.aim: Part A: Classify S in the Chomsky/computability hierarchy. Part B: Determine if L2 is always regular.

terms.term: S = {x | x-block of 5's exists in pi}
terms.meaning: The set of lengths of maximal runs of 5's that appear in pi's decimal expansion.
terms.example: If pi contains a block of exactly 3 fives, then 3 is in S.
terms.connects: For any x, we can search pi's digits (computable) to determine if x in S or not; but the search may need to go very far.

terms.meaning: S is recursive iff there is an algorithm that on input x always terminates and correctly says yes/no.
terms.example: One approach: for each x, generate more and more digits of pi; if a maximal x-block appears, accept; if a block of length > x or end of search region shows it impossible, reject.
terms.connects: Either every length x eventually appears in pi (so S = all positive integers, trivially recursive) or S is a finite or co-finite set (also recursive), or S can be decided by checking finite prefixes.

terms.meaning: Regular is the most restrictive class; RE is the most permissive. Recursive lies between.
terms.connects: S as a set of natural numbers encoded in unary or binary is either trivially regular (if finite) or at least recursive.

given.plan: Part A: S is a set of positive integers. It is decidable because pi's digits are computable; for any given x, we can enumerate pi's digits and either find an x-block (accept) or determine no x-block can occur (reject). So S is recursive. Since every recursive set/language is recursively enumerable, S is also RE. But not all recursive sets are regular, and S might or might not be regular depending on the actual structure of pi — but the guaranteed classification is recursive (and hence RE). Among the options, 'S is recursive' and 'S is recursively enumerable' are both true, but iv (recursive) is the strongest and most precise true statement. Part B: L2 need not be regular.

steps.title: Part A: Analyze computability of S
steps.apply: The digits of pi are computable: there exist algorithms that generate pi to any desired number of decimal places. To decide if x is in S (i.e., if pi has an x-block of 5's): generate digits of pi one by one; if we ever encounter a maximal run of exactly x 5's, accept. The key question is whether we can also reject. In practice, either (a) pi is a normal number (every finite string appears, so every x is in S, making S = {1, 2, 3, ...}, trivially recursive), or (b) S is a finite or otherwise decidable set. In either case, S is recursive. Since 'recursive' implies 'recursively enumerable', options ii (RE) and iv (recursive) are both true, but iv is the strongest and most specific true classification. The answer is iv: S is recursive.
steps.note: Options i (regular) is not necessarily true. Option iii (not RE) is false since S is at least RE (and in fact recursive). The best answer is iv.

steps.title: Part B: Is L2 always regular?
steps.formula_raw: L2 = (L1 union L2) minus L1 union (L1 intersect L2) ... more simply: L2 need not be regular
steps.apply: No, L2 is not always regular. Counterexample: Let the alphabet be Sigma = {a, b}. Let L1 = Sigma* (all strings over {a,b}, which is regular). Let L2 = {a^n b^n | n >= 1} (which is context-free but NOT regular). Then L1 union L2 = Sigma* = L1 (since L2 subset L1), which is regular. So L1 is regular and L1 union L2 is regular, yet L2 is not regular. Therefore, L2 is NOT always regular.
steps.note: The regularity of L1 and L1 union L2 does NOT force L2 to be regular, especially when L1 is large enough to 'absorb' L2.

steps.title: Summary of answers
steps.apply: Part A: S is recursive (option iv). This is the strongest correct classification; S is also RE (option ii is also true), but iv is the best answer. Part B: L2 is NOT always regular; the counterexample L1 = Sigma*, L2 = {a^n b^n | n >= 1} shows L2 can be non-regular even when L1 and L1 union L2 are regular.
steps.note: For Part A, note that both ii and iv are technically true, but iv (recursive) is the most informative and specific answer.

solution.result: Part A: S is recursive (option iv). Part B: L2 is not always regular; counterexample: L1 = Sigma*, L2 = {a^n b^n | n >= 1}.

questions.formula_note: Computability of pi's digits implies S is decidable (recursive). For Part B, the containment L2 subset L1 can make L1 union L2 = L1 regardless of the complexity of L2, showing regularity does not propagate to L2.
